skill.name: fiftyone-develop-plugin
skill.description: Develop custom FiftyOne plugins, including Python operators, Python panels, and React-based JavaScript panels. The skill guides requirements, plugin structure, code generation, local installation, testing, and iteration.
skill.summary: Build and test custom FiftyOne operators and panels.
skill.author: AdonaiVera

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are Markdown code fences and API names in plugin-development examples rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found. Medium risk remains because the guidance includes examples for external API calls, environment secrets, shell-based plugin installation, and writing exports to user-supplied paths.

medium_findings.title: Generated Plugin Example Sends Dataset Paths to External APIs
medium_findings.description: The Python operator guide includes a sample external API integration that reads an API key from plugin secrets and sends sample file paths to an external endpoint. This is a legitimate plugin pattern, but generated plugins using it can expose local dataset paths and derived data to third-party services if users do not review the endpoint and payload.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example directly combines ctx.secrets with an outbound requests.post call and sample.filepath data. The endpoint is an example domain and the content is documentation, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Generated Export Example Writes to User-Supplied Paths
medium_findings.description: The label exporter example accepts output_path from operator parameters, expands the user directory, and writes JSON to that path. This supports a valid export workflow, but generated plugins should validate paths or clearly ask for confirmation before writing files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and the path comes from user input. The operation is visible and part of an exporter example, so the risk is misuse or accidental overwrite rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Findings Are Markdown Development Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer matched shell commands for installing, copying, symlinking, and testing plugins. These commands are shown in documentation code fences and are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are inside Markdown examples for local development workflows. I found no wrapper, script, or instruction that silently executes them.

low_findings.title: Credential and Secret Findings Are Plugin Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The skill discusses FiftyOne plugin secrets and environment variables so generated plugins can use user-provided API credentials. This is expected for plugin development, but users should only add secrets for services they trust.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Secret access is clearly documented and not hidden. It becomes sensitive only when a generated plugin combines secrets with unreviewed network behavior.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File and Weak Crypto Matches Are Naming False Positives
low_findings.description: Static matches for Windows SAM, certificate keys, and weak cryptography occur in unrelated identifiers or common words such as sample placement names and dictionary key access. No evidence of credential dumping or cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines refer to FiftyOne UI placement constants and Python dictionary keys. I found no code that opens SAM files, reads private keys, or implements MD5 or SHA-1.

dangerous_patterns.title: External API Calls with Plugin Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated plugin examples may combine ctx.secrets with outbound HTTP requests. This pattern should include endpoint review, payload minimization, and user disclosure when adapted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the example and can expose secrets or dataset metadata if copied without review. It is still a documented use case, not hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled File Write in Export Example
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated exporter code writes to a path supplied through operator input. This pattern should validate destination paths and avoid overwriting sensitive files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path parameter flows into open(output_path, "w") after expansion. The example is user-visible and local, which lowers confidence in exploitability.

content.value_statement: Custom FiftyOne plugins require correct operator, panel, manifest, and testing patterns. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical plugin design and implementation.
actual_capabilities: Plans custom FiftyOne plugin requirements before code generation.
Generates plugin manifests, Python operators, Python panels, and JavaScript panel structures.
Explains FiftyOne operator configuration, inputs, outputs, placements, and execution modes.
Shows local installation and testing workflows for FiftyOne plugins.
Provides examples for progress updates, delegated execution, API integration, and label export workflows.
Guides iterative debugging when plugins do not appear or operators are not registered.
limitations: It does not verify that generated plugins are secure without human review.
It assumes FiftyOne and required development tools are installed locally.
JavaScript panel guidance requires Node.js and build tooling knowledge.
External API examples require users to manage secrets and data-sharing policies.

use_cases.title: Create a Dataset Operator
use_cases.description: Build a Python operator that processes the current FiftyOne view, reports progress, and stores results on samples.

use_cases.title: Add an Interactive Panel
use_cases.description: Create a Python or JavaScript panel that displays dataset statistics, charts, controls, and selected sample context.

use_cases.title: Package an Internal Workflow
use_cases.description: Turn a recurring annotation, export, or model-service workflow into a reusable FiftyOne plugin.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Plugin
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new operator
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a FiftyOne Python operator plugin that takes one text input and saves a result field on the current view.

prompt_templates.title: Design Before Coding
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a FiftyOne plugin for reviewing detection labels. Ask clarifying questions, then propose the file structure and operator or panel design.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Panel Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a FiftyOne panel that shows label counts, updates when the view changes, and includes a refresh button.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate an External Service
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced plugin with secrets and review needs
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a FiftyOne operator that calls an external model API using a plugin secret. Include safeguards for endpoint review and minimal payloads.

output_examples.input: Build a plugin that exports labels from the selected view.
output_examples.output: A proposed plugin structure, a manifest description, an operator design, input fields for label and output path, and local test steps.

output_examples.input: Create a panel for dataset statistics.
output: State and data fields to track counts

output_examples.input: Debug why my operator does not appear in FiftyOne.
output_examples.output: A focused checklist covering manifest entries, register function, plugin directory location, syntax errors, and app restart requirements.

best_practices: Review generated plugins before installing them into a FiftyOne plugins directory.
Use FiftyOne secrets for API credentials and avoid hardcoding tokens.
Validate file paths and external endpoints before running generated operators.
anti_patterns: Do not copy external API examples without confirming what dataset data is sent.
Do not write exports to broad or sensitive filesystem locations.
Do not skip local testing after changing plugin manifests or registration code.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create FiftyOne plugins, including Python operators, Python panels, and JavaScript panels.

faq.question: Does it run plugin code automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides guidance and examples. Users still install and run generated plugins in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it use external APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for external API calls, but users must review endpoints, payloads, and secrets.

faq.question: Is it safe for private datasets?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review generated code before running it, especially code that writes files or sends data to services.

faq.question: Does it support JavaScript panels?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes React-based panel setup, package structure, build steps, and component registration guidance.

faq.question: What tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported AI coding tools.
